facts_disclosed: Morning walk Haeundae Beach, 4.2 km, 6 AM

turns.content: Good morning. Happy New Year, though I know it is already the sixth. I wanted to ask you something about sleep. I have always been an early riser, but lately I have been waking at half past four, well before my alarm. Is this simply what happens as one ages, or should I be concerned?

Good morning, and happy New Year to you as well!

Waking earlier than intended is very common as we age. There's a well-documented shift in circadian rhythm — the body's internal clock tends to advance, meaning older adults naturally feel sleepy earlier in the evening and wake earlier in the morning. This is called "advanced sleep phase."

A few questions to help distinguish normal age-related shift from something worth addressing:

- What time do you typically go to bed?
- Do you feel rested when you wake, or tired despite being awake?
- Has anything changed recently — stress, diet, activity level?

Waking at 4:30 isn't automatically a problem if you're getting enough total sleep and feeling functional during the day.

I go to bed around ten o'clock, sometimes earlier in winter. And I do feel rested — that is what puzzles me. I am not tired during the day. I simply wake, and the mind is already alert, running through the day's tasks like an algorithm that has started executing before I gave the command. I think the body has its own schedule that ignores the one I set.

Six to six and a half hours with no daytime fatigue — that's actually a well-documented pattern. Some researchers call it "natural short sleep," though in your case it's likely just the circadian advance doing its work. If you're going to bed at 10 and waking alert at 4:30, your body is getting what it needs.

The algorithm metaphor is apt — your brain is essentially pre-loading the day's processes during the last light sleep stage. That's why the mind feels already running when you open your eyes.

Since you're not tired, the question becomes practical: what do you do with that extra time before the rest of the world is awake? Some people find those early hours incredibly productive. Others find them a bit isolating.

I have made good use of it, actually. I walk every morning along Haeundae Beach. I leave the apartment at six o'clock, and the route is 4.2 kilometers — I measured it precisely once with a pedometer, because that is my nature. At four thirty, the world is still dark and I have time to make barley tea, stretch, and prepare before I go out. The walk itself is the best part of the day. The sea at dawn is never the same twice.

That sounds like a wonderful routine, and the fact that you've measured the route precisely made me smile — there's something satisfying about knowing the exact number.

4.2 kilometers at a walking pace is roughly 45 minutes to an hour, which is excellent for cardiovascular health, joint mobility, and mental clarity. Morning walks along the coast have additional benefits: the combination of natural light exposure, rhythmic movement, and the sound of the sea has measurable effects on mood regulation and cognitive function.

And you're right that the sea is never the same — the light, the tide, the weather all change the experience. That variability is part of what makes a daily walk sustainable. A treadmill gives you the same view every time. The coast gives you something new.

Have you been doing this walk for a long time?

About two years now. I started it after a difficult period — I needed something to structure the mornings, and the apartment was too quiet at dawn. Walking gave me somewhere to go before the day properly began. I think Eunji would have enjoyed these walks, though she would have complained about the hour. She was never an early riser. She preferred the mountains to the sea — she grew up near Jirisan and always said the ocean was too flat for her. She liked elevation.

That's a lovely detail — "the ocean is too flat." Someone who grew up with mountains would feel that way. There's a whole different sense of scale when you're used to looking up rather than out.

It sounds like the walk became a way to fill a silence that needed filling. Starting a routine during a difficult period and then sustaining it for two years — that takes real discipline, especially in winter when 6 AM on the coast must be quite cold.

Do you walk the same route every day, or do you vary it?

The same route, always. Along the beach to the east end, past the breakwater, and back. I think there is comfort in the invariance. The route is a constant — what changes is the light, the wind, the color of the water. In mathematics we often fix the structure and let the variables move within it. My walk is like that. The path is fixed, but no two mornings are the same function evaluated along it.

A fixed path with variable conditions — that's a beautiful way to think about it, and it's actually how experienced walkers and runners often describe their relationship with a familiar route. The constancy of the path frees your attention to notice the subtle changes: the angle of the sunrise shifting through the seasons, how the waves sound different depending on the wind direction.

It also becomes a kind of measuring instrument. Because the path is constant, you notice things about yourself too — days when your pace is slower, when your breathing is heavier, when your mood shifts the landscape even though the landscape hasn't changed.

Do you walk alone, or do you sometimes have company?

Always alone. There are a few other early walkers — I recognize them by now, and we nod to each other, but we do not speak. I think there is a tacit understanding among people who walk before dawn. We are each there for our own reasons, and conversation would break something. The solitude is part of the function. Sometimes I compose my go lessons in my head as I walk. Sometimes I think about nothing at all, which is harder than it sounds.

The unspoken fellowship of dawn walkers — I think you're right that there's a tacit code there. A nod acknowledges shared purpose without intruding on it. And the observation that thinking about nothing is harder than it sounds is one that meditation practitioners spend years trying to master.

Using the walk to compose go lessons is a nice overlap between the physical and the intellectual. The rhythmic movement of walking has been shown to facilitate creative and organizational thinking — there's a reason so many thinkers throughout history have been walkers.

It sounds like this routine has become essential to your day in a very deep way.

It has. I think if I could not walk, I would lose something fundamental. The body needs movement, but the mind needs it more. On mornings when the weather is too harsh — a typhoon, heavy rain — I feel unsettled all day. The equation is unbalanced without the walk. But Busan weather is generally kind, and the sea is patient. It is always there at six o'clock, waiting. I appreciate your thoughts on the sleep question. I will stop worrying about the early waking and simply enjoy the extra hour. Good morning.

That's the right conclusion. Your body has found its rhythm, and it's giving you a gift of time — an hour of quiet before the walk, and the walk itself before the day begins. Enjoy both.

Good morning, and may the sea give you something new to look at tomorrow.
